plan: Sell all leveraged ETFs to eliminate that risk.
Trim semiconductor and AI/Software exposure to reduce concentration.
Reduce broad index (SPY) to meet single-name limits.
Add to income ETF (QQQI) for stability and yield.
Buy a high-quality, strong setup name (AAPL) for potential growth.
conservative.read: This plan prioritizes capital preservation with a target of around 1.5% monthly return. It significantly reduces leverage and concentration risk, holding a larger cash buffer for stability and lower potential drawdowns.

plan: Trim leveraged ETFs, keeping only a small position in SOXL.
Reduce exposure in broad index (SPY) and semiconductors (SMH).
Slightly trim AI/Software (CRWD) to manage concentration.
Add new, strong setup names (AVGO, LEU) for growth potential.
Maintain a moderate cash buffer and balanced sector exposure.
balanced.read: This plan aims for risk-first growth, targeting around 2.5% monthly return. It balances broad market exposure with quality tech and limited leverage, while reducing overall concentration and maintaining a cash buffer.

plan: Reduce broad index (SPY) and income (QQQI) exposure.
Increase concentration in semiconductors (SMH) and AI/Software (CRWD).
Add to leveraged semiconductor ETF (SOXL) for amplified gains.
Buy new, high-scoring setup names (GUSH, AVAV) for aggressive growth.
Maintain a small cash buffer, accepting higher potential drawdowns.
aggressive.read: This aggressive plan targets significant growth, aiming for around 4% monthly return, but accepts much higher risk and potential drawdowns. It leans into leverage and sector concentration, understanding the amplified gains and losses, including leveraged ETF decay.

plan: Sell all leveraged ETFs and high-beta tech/semis.
Reduce broad index (SPY) exposure.
Significantly increase allocation to income ETF (QQQI).
Add a stable, dividend-paying name (XOM) for income.
Prioritize steady monthly distributions and capital preservation.
income.read: This income-focused plan targets around 1% monthly return through distributions, prioritizing stability and lower drawdowns. It rotates out of growth and leverage into income-generating assets, accepting lower growth potential for more reliable income.
